data.title: HF Foods: New Numbers, Old Problems
data.description:
Ever since that first-quarter pop, it looks like HF Foods (HFFG) has been running out of magic to impress the market.

That's not by chance. Being one of the top Asian food suppliers in the country, HF Foods is still struggling with two big headaches: weak traffic and tariffs.

Not much has changed in the big picture lately, but if you've been keeping up with my past pieces, you've probably noticed it too. Restaurant traffic started cooling off toward the end of the quarter.

That seems to be my biggest disagreement with the CEO.

There's still the impact from tariffs in terms of inventory, pricing and certainly, foot traffic. Beginning of Q3, we saw it continue to be a little bit softer, but it rebounded nicely towards the end of Q3.

You don’t need to look far to spot this trend. The NRA’s Restaurant Performance Index tells the same story, backing up the numbers I’ve been showing you.

In Q3, the RPI never left negative territory, sliding 0.4% in September and ending the quarter a bit rough.

Looking under the hood, ‘Customer Traffic’ took the hardest hit, dropping 1.3% month over month. And it didn't stop there—every single piece of the Current Situation Index, which basically tracks what's happening right now on the ground, also moved lower.

With ‘Customer Traffic’ down to 98.2, we're pretty much back to where we were in March and April. It’s not the disaster we saw in February, but that steady climb folks were talking about seems to have lost grip.

Now, the effect of this is obvious. With fewer customers at the restaurants, less food will be sold and, consequently, HF Foods will distribute fewer goods.

As I said earlier, FY 2025 and maybe even 2026 look like investment years, with a focus on easing these pressures and running things more efficiently.

Here’s a quick reminder of the investments they’ve got going on:

  1. More private labels are coming out of the gate (the oil bottling operation in Kansas City is one of them) part of the plan to do more on their own and boost margins.
  2. They're also spreading their bets when it comes to sourcing. Shrimp now comes from Peru, Chile, and Ecuador, and they’ve added suppliers in New Zealand and Canada to ease the tariff pain.
  3. An ERP system’s now running company-wide, and they’ve tightened up the sales team, cutting it down and training the rest to work sharper.
  4. And on the logistics side, the Kirkland site in Atlanta nearly doubled its space, while the Great Wall Seafood warehouse in Chicago cut rental costs.

The aim is to narrow the margin gap with larger, well-established peers such as Sysco (SYY), US Foods (USFD) and Performance Food Group (PFGC), all of which have the size to manage customer bankruptcies and cushion tariff impacts.

But as you can see, they've still got a long way to go:

If you followed my advice and went with the big players, you're doing a lot better than anyone who bet on HF Foods.

Turns out what I said aged like a good bottle left in the cellar:

Blue chips are basically the safe haven right now in this industry. They’ve got everything going for them: a large, diversified client base, a massive distribution network, and plenty of tools to deal with inflation, tariffs, or whatever new trade war pops up next Tuesday.

Incremental Gains, Limited Impact

HF Foods managed triple-digit profit growth, although the quarter was still a bit of a mixed story in Wall Street's eyes.

Sales came in a bit stronger than expected at $307 million, a 2.9% increase from a year ago, despite all the talk about slowing orders. EPS came in at $0.08, missing estimates by $0.04, although it still doubled from a year ago.

Even so, the results did not impress the market at all, and the stock fell by a mid-single digit percentage to ~$2.30, the closest it has ever been to its 52-week low.

As the CFO explained, they saw a tailwind from stronger order volumes and a little help from pricing, mostly in the meat, poultry, and seafood categories, which are still pretty inflated.

Well, if we take a look at the numbers coming out of spots like Kura Sushi (KRUS), GEN Korean BBQ (GENK), and Haidilao (HDL), traffic’s still heading south, even as prices keep climbing.

I’d say pricing likely did most of the heavy lifting for HF Foods.

Most margins got a little lift this quarter, helped by SG&A coming in a bit lighter versus revenue and lower interest expenses. The only one that didn't play along was the gross margin, since seafood and other low-margin items took up a bigger slice of the mix.

Just take a look at this:

I can't help but notice HF Foods had to rely on extra debt this quarter to offset the OCF hit from growing operating assets.

This is a problem, and a big one.

HF Foods has $167.5 million in short- and long-term debt (Debt/EBITDA of 4.3x) and, generating unstable cash flow, the company could be at the mercy of payment terms.

All in all, even with the stronger bottom line, it's pretty clear HF Foods is still stuck with the same issues—slow growth, thin margins, and shaky cash flow.

Being the biggest name in the Asian restaurant niche still counts for something, just not as much as it used to.

Still Cheap For A Reason?

Since management hasn't provided any updates on what to expect for FY 2025, I'll stick with my previous base case scenario (consensus revenue of $1.2 billion and EBITDA between $37 and $45 million).

But instead of stacking HF Foods up against the blue chips, let's see how it measures up to other beaten-down names.

For example, we can use United Natural Foods (UNFI), which recently closed its Fort Wayne facility and has similar margins. Others like Mission Produce (AVO) and G. Willi-Food (WILC) also seem like interesting comparisons.

In this case, the group is trading at around 8.9x EBITDA on average.

With the midpoint of this range at approximately $40 million, I'm targeting an EV of $356 million. Excluding net debt ($225.1 million) and dividing by the 53 million shares outstanding, we would arrive at a price target close to $2.40.

This is ~20% below my previous price target of $3!

Now, with the stock hovering around $2.20, there's only a mid-to-high single-digit upside. Not exactly something to get me fired up about HF Foods right now.

That said, I'm sticking with a 'Hold' on this one. I see fair value topping out around $3, since the earnings show they might still have one more solid quarter up their sleeve.

Still, it's a shaky setup with plenty of risks, so I'd rather just watch from the sidelines.

Market Review

Global equity markets advanced in the third quarter, building on gains from the previous period. Bullish talking points included a shift toward Federal Reserve (Fed) dovishness with a rate cut delivered in September, better-than-expected corporate earnings and forward guidance, continued generative artificial intelligence growth tailwinds, and economic data resilience with consumer spending remaining firm. However, policy uncertainty remained the primary headwind over the period as US-China trade talks continued, though broader deals were announced with the EU and Japan. Additionally, the Fed maintained its focus on labor market conditions amid mixed employment data, with job creation slowing over recent months.1

  • Security selection within the Information Technology and Materials sectors were the largest contributors to performance. The Fund’s position in Lam Research Corporation (LRCX) ( 1. 40% ), which engages in the design, manufacture, refurbishment, and provision of semiconductor processing equipment, contributed to relative performance. The company reported a 25% increase in revenue and a 33% increase in earnings. This was driven by increasing demand for AI and datacenter chips.
  • The Fund’s position in CRH public limited company (CRH) ( 2. 56% ), which manufactures and distributes building materials and products for the construction industry, contributed to relative performance. The company posted strong second quarter revenue and net income with expanded margins. The company agreed to acquire Eco Material Technologies for $2.1 billion, enhancing its position in low-carbon cementitious materials.
  • Conversely, security selection within the Energy and Financials sectors were the largest detractors from performance. The Fund’s position in Cheniere Energy Inc. (LNG) ( 1.92%), which engages in liquefied natural gas related businesses, detracted from relative performance. While geopolitical tensions and oversupply concerns have hurt recent performance, the company highlighted growing demand, strategic partnerships, and their dividend growth target of ~10% annually as reasons to stay positive.
  • The Fund’s position in Marsh & McLennan Companies, Inc. (MMC) ( 1.89%), which engages in risk consulting services, also detracted from relative performance. Shares fell as segment performance was mixed. The company cited geopolitical and economic uncertainty that affected client decision-making.

Portfolio positioning & outlook

Despite earlier uncertainty during the beginning of the year, the equity markets continue to be resilient. At one point this year, the S&P 500 was down 15%, and now it is nearly up 15%, highlighting the volatility that investors have experienced. Taking a step back and evaluating the overall market: overall market trends are still intact, inflation remains below 3%, which is historically positive for stocks; on the liquidity front, credit spreads remain tame, the yield of the 10-year Treasury has been range bound, the Fed recently cut rates by 25 bps and there may be another before the end of the year, and lastly earnings remain healthy and are expected to continue broaden out beyond the Magnificent 7.

Following a value-driven market regime spanning over 30 years, and a subsequent 12+ year period favoring growth, the current environment provides a favorable backdrop for quality-focused investing, which is integral to our value investment process. As we look ahead, many areas of theU. S. economy are seeing differentiated growth relative to the broader market, and we are continuing to find quality companies across the market cap spectrum. We continue to focus on these companies, as we believe their expanding markets, combined with market-share gains, pricing power, and efficiency-driven margin expansion, support above average revenue and earnings growth relative to peers and the broader market. In our view, quality businesses that have relatively favorable shareholder yields, a high degree of earnings predictability, high returns, and strong cash flow generation have the agility to help them adapt to today’s increasingly uncertain environment, invest more strategically in their businesses, and seek out attractive M&A opportunities to enhance their existing businesses. We are also optimistic that smaller capitalized stocks can begin to outperform as earnings growth accelerates after a long period of underperformance to large caps.

We continue to see opportunities across several long-term themes including U.S. infrastructure spending, a long-term U. S. housing shortage, offshore energy development, and companies that enable the widespread adoption of artificial intelligence (AI).

  • Companies that could benefit from U.S. infrastructure spending include engineering & construction firms, industrial companies, building materials and building products companies.
  • The secular U.S. housing shortage presents opportunities for homebuilders, building materials and building products, rental housing, and home improvement companies.
  • Offshore energy development offers growth prospects for major oil companies engaged in offshore exploration & development, and offshore service providers.
  • Generative AI is a widely discussed and debated theme in the markets today. The potential for AI to benefit various industries is extensive. While the most evident advantages may be seen in businesses directly involved with AI, there are also significant opportunities in sectors that indirectly support and scale the technology, such as chip manufacturers to utility companies and their vendors that are racing to build the incremental capacity to support this revolution.

data.title: Onto Innovation: Accelerating Momentum Into 2026
data.description:
In the summer, I believed that Onto Innovation (ONTO) was putting cash productively back to work, announcing an apparently interesting acquisition of Semilab. Following that deal, the third quarter earnings report was a bit soft, yet momentum seems to reappear in the fourth quarter and into 2026, driving momentum in the shares in recent weeks and months.

While this is to be applauded, that move higher has elevated expectations a bit as well, to levels at which I do not necessarily see real appeal here. This makes me cautious to chase the shares here but keen to keep a close eye on developments from here onwards.

Process Control For Semiconductor Applications

Onto Innovation describes its own business as a capital equipment company, which develops and produces optical process solutions for semiconductor and related markets. Such products and applications focus on improvements in performance, quality, yield, and reliability, thereby helping the entire industry in driving further advancements and acceleration of technological developments.

The company provides such services, similar to industry leaders like KLA Corp. (KLAC) and Applied Materials (AMAT), yet Onto is much smaller, but this gives it a longer runway for growth as well. Over the past decade, the company has quadrupled sales to levels around the billion mark, although that part of this growth resulted from the merger between its predecessors: Rudolph and Nanometrics, causing about 50% dilution in the share base.

Nonetheless, a stock trading in the mere teens in the mid 2010s first hit the $100 mark in 2021, peaked near the $250 mark in 2024, and fell back to the $100 mark over the summer.

Valuation Thoughts

After a temporary boom in 2022, the business has been struggling in recent years, yet growth returned last year. In February of this year, the company reported a 21% increase in fourth quarter sales to $264 million, with revenues trending over a billion per annum again.

The company reported first quarter sales at $267 million, with adjusted earnings reported at $1.51 per share. The company only guided for second quarter earnings at around a quarter of a billion, with some sequential pressure on earnings seen as well.

Trading around the $100 mark over the summer, there were some developments, as the company announced a $545 million deal for Semilab International, adding $130 million in revenues derived from inline wafer contamination monitoring and materials interface characterization.

Valued at 4.2 times sales, this multiple was in line with Onto’s valuation at around the $100 mark, although that these acquired assets have seen 20% compounded annual growth rates since 2021.

With the business posting earnings close to $5 per share, the comment that 10% earnings per share accretion was seen, and net cash balances remain post the deal, all this looked quite compelling.

A Recovery

Since the summer, shares of Onto have gradually risen to the $150 mark, having fallen to $133 per share on the back of some jittery in the wider technology sector in recent weeks, and after the company did not really beat on low third quarter expectations.

Early in July, Onto reported second quarter sales at $253 million and change, up 5% on the year before. Adjusted earnings of $1.25 per share were down seven cents on the year before, with that number looking quite clean, i.e. not adjusted for stock-based compensation expenses. The company issued a downbeat guidance for the third quarter, seeing revenues down to just $210-$225 million, with non-GAAP earnings seen around $0.85 per share.

Third quarter results were just in line with the guidance, as third quarter sales of $218 million were down 13% on the year before, with adjusted earnings of $0.92 per share, being a touch stronger than guided for. Net cash holdings rose to just shy of a billion, all ahead of the Semilab purchase, of course. The third quarter revenue dip is seen ending in the fourth quarter, with sales seen between $250-$265 million, with adjusted earnings seen between $1.18-$1.33 per share, but frankly this is in line with the performance in the first half of the year, despite raging booming markets here in the wider end markets.

With 49 million shares trading at $133, this values equity of the business at $6.5 billion, or $5.5 billion if we factor in net cash (equal to $20 per share ahead of the deal for Semilab) for an earnings multiple in the low-twenties, as growth has been a bit underwhelming this year. However, on the conference call, the company indicated that some 20% growth might be in the works in 2026, growing sequentially throughout the year.

Note that the company sees closing of the Semilab purchase in a couple of weeks, however this contribution is not factored into the guidance for the fourth quarter. With its revenue run rate exceeding $10 million per month, and the purchase contribution about a month to this year's results, there is some upside to the near term results. Moreover, the purchase price has been reduced to $495 million, a fifty million reduction, without much of an explanation given for doing so, something which can be both a positive and negative of course.

And Now?

In between all this, investors should be setting themselves up for solid growth next year, and that is key. With pro forma net cash holdings down to about $10 per share, unleveraged assets trade at $123 per share, while earnings might advance a long way to $6 per share next year, in between organic growth and the Semilab contribution.

I like this set-up, but some momentum has been seen in the share price already, which makes me hesitant to get involved at current levels, but I am keen to keep a close eye on this company and team, hoping for better entry levels over time.

data.title: Siemens Healthineers: Healthcare Remains Attractive After 2025
data.description:
In an inflated market, like the one I believe we currently have, finding value or undervaluation is never the easiest thing. I would go so far as to say it becomes almost impossible after a while, not because some companies are no longer cheap, but because even the cheap companies, in effect, are going to fall when the market does. That means that at a certain point, which of course is yours to determine, it's better to hold money in risk-free methods than to invest it. When that time comes will depend on your individual circumstances, as well as the tax-adjusted risk-free rate you may get for your money where you are.

For me, the rate is so-so. I can get 2-3% risk-free easily, but not much more (which is a lot less than some). It means that my picks, as the market keeps climbing, become fewer and fewer.

It's also the reason I now hold 37% cash in my portfolio, which I have not done since I started investing. There are personal reasons for this as well (some off-market investments), but I would not hold this much cash in any case if I did not see significant risk.

So, it means something when I say that I don't see a significant risk in Siemens Healthineers (OTCPK:SEMHF). This is obviously a company that I've covered and invested in for some time. My position is now just above 1%, and I expect it to grow to 1.5% over time as I add more, slowly.

The company reported its 4Q25 results about a week ago, from the time of submission of this article. I therefore view this as a good opportunity for an update - and to show you why I remain positive on what I consider a very qualitative business.

Let's see what we have here. You can find my last article on the company here.

Siemens Healthineers - Focus on growth, efficiency, and margins

The reason I consider this update quite important is due to the significant share price downturn after the 4Q25/FY25 period. The company fell from close to €50/share native to well below €44/share.

Let's begin by looking at the reason for this - because several buy alerts were triggered on my side, and I immediately bought shares at that time, in line with my overall thesis and recommendation/rating for the business.

At first glance, it's hard to tell what the problem is. The company saw strong equipment growth, and that growth came in from almost every geography. Comparable revenue growth was at almost 6%, which was not only well within the company's guidance but at the upper end of the guidance. The same was true for EPS.

Margin expansion was, in fact, another positive. Imaging, Varian, and AT, all of the important segments, saw significant growth in margin expansion due to scaling, HSD growth, and transformation. All of this was despite challenges in the company's one negative area, China.

If you wanted to argue for what's driving things down, you'd really have to start by arguing things like tariffs (which I believe to be a risk, but something that is developing, rather than permanent). Driving Healthineers down here implies disregarding entirely the company's confirmed underlying margin growth (ex tariffs), as well as the revenue growth.

Imaging as a segment, especially, saw strong trends, with revenue growth above 6% with very strong growth in PETNET and other areas. In other articles, I covered the company's Diagnostics segment. This segment has long been one of the company's core issues, which is also why Healthineers has been targeting a transformation that has been ongoing for some time. Revenue is not yet in the bag in terms of growth, but the margin saw a nice upswing in this quarter.

The company's main peers, and also therefore competition as well as alternative investments, include businesses such as Philips, Abbott (ABT), and Swedish Elekta - although I would consider Elekta to be a niche player here, with little comparable appeal and significant volatility that makes it a hard investment, despite the arguably extremely low valuation for the Swedish peer (more on peers further below in valuation).

Healthineers, on the other hand, is a global giant, owned over 70% by conglomerate Siemens (OTCPK:SIEGY), and has one of the most fundamentally attractive portfolios on the market.

The company is backed by Siemens credit, which is A-rated; it has a market cap of close to €50B, and an estimated forward growth rate of more than 8.5% in AEPS for the coming 3 fiscals, albeit with a dip expected in 2026E, after the 7% AEPS growth in 2025A, as we see here.

That's the interesting thing. Despite growth, despite all the positives, the company still dropped in trading and is only slowly recovering now. If we go through the quarterly results one by one in 2025, we see results exceeding expectations early in the year, a strong 2Q, with raises in 3Q and several upsides. However, tariff impacts were the "talk of the town" during many of the quarters, as well as problems with the Chinese market (stagnation, similar to peer Carl Zeiss (OTCPK:CZMWY). This led to an over 12% share price crash during earnings day, which is what I took advantage of and consider a massive overreaction.

It is my firm opinion, with high conviction, that 2025A was a good year for Healthineers. Tariffs are not something the group can do more about (than it's already doing), and I've already included significant tariff impacts in my PT and FV estimates. I am not including more. The biggest unknowns for Siemens Healthineers, and therefore the biggest risks in 2026E to 2028E, are three.

First, the Chinese market. When, how, and if it will recover. The visibility for this is poor, due above all to increased local competition. This is a real big one.

Secondly, the tariff situation. We'll see how this develops, but as I said, I've included it in my estimates already. I will not be making further adjustments because of it.

Third, and last, the diagnostics transformation. It's ongoing, but again, the visibility for it is poor. It's a multi-year program, and Diagnostics has, for a long time, had issues with profitability. It's my expectation that this will improve, but given inflation, stagnating GDP growth in most of the company's major markets, and other geopolitical factors, time will tell exactly how much the company can turn this around to profit.

However, I consider 2025A a very solid year that confirms the company's premiumization on the market - let's look at what this means for valuation.

Valuation - Upside is increasingly likely at this price, even with market inflation

This company tends to, and still as of today, trade/s at a premium. We have 18.6x P/E normalized for the native ticker SHL, which is what I tend to invest in and look at. The ADR is SEMHF, and I will provide a PT for this one as well.

Your view on Siemens Healthineers really depends on how appealing you think the premiumization is. Because good companies in this peer group and sector tend toward a very high premium - Abbott, for instance, averages 25x P/E - I consider it clear that the market and investors accept a premium for these companies.

However, and obviously so, the lower that premium is, and we can still get market-beating returns, the better your investment safety is.

So, at this time, you can get 15% annualized, which is my hurdle rate - inclusive of the 2.14% dividend from SHL if you buy it at a 21x P/E. How good is that? Well, the company's 10-year average is 24x P/E. The company's 5-year average is 23x. So, an upside at 21x P/E to 15% annualized is a considerable upside with a discount, I would argue, since the upside is 20% annualized at the 5-year average.

I consider the company fairly valued at the 23x P/E on a forward basis. Before, I've implied a fair value at about €71-€73 at most. I maintain that as of this time. This means that my PT of €53/share, which is what I assigned more clearly in my last article, still stands here. I am neither raising nor downgrading it for 2025E, because results were in line not only with company expectations but also with my own expectations as well.

The forecasted decline in 2026E is a minor issue in my view. We may see the company cheaper than now, but even if we do, it doesn't change my long-term thesis. If your conviction is as high as mine is, then this company could potentially be a very solid buy here.

Alternatives may be appealing, but they come with different risk profiles.

Abbott Labs could have upside, but at a higher P/E. The yield is lower as well - so I say "no" here. The upside, even at 25-26x P/E, is less than 14%, so it doesn't clear my hurdle. Carl Zeiss Meditec could be compared, and I do invest in this company as well. However, I would not be doing my job as an analyst with experience if I didn't tell you in no uncertain terms that this company is only a tenth the size of Healthineers and has a much different volatility and short-term risk profile. That is, even though I technically view it as a superior investment in terms of real upside, even to a heavily discounted average.

In closing, I continue to rate Siemens Healthineers as "BUY" and as one of the most attractive (relative to others in risk/upside) investments in this sector. I maintain a PT of €53 and an FV of €72/share, meaning the price at which I would consider rotation of my shares.

Risks and Considerations

The primary risk to this company is one of valuation (still in 2025A). We can still avoid this one if we buy at €46, but less so if we buy at €50, and definitely not (in my view) if we buy at €53/share or above.

However, that's not a problem at the price below €45/share we're seeing now.

It's my view that every indicator and model points to a long-term fair value of between €65 and €73. Above this would be tricky. I don't expect this.

Operational risks for the company are few and far between, but keeping an eye on the diagnostics turnaround and the APAC segment is a good idea for value-conscious investors. I've mentioned this before, and I mention it again here, as other risks are not something the company is significantly in control of (geopoliticals and the like).

At this time, Siemens Healthineers continues to be a Buy. The target for the ADR, SEMHF, comes to $61.34 as of the latest FX.

Thesis

Healthineers is among a class-leading group of companies in the medtech/equipment sector. While not the highest yield available, it nonetheless presents an appealing thesis, with the potential for a double-digit upside at a parent's credit safety of A+/A1. This is worth noting and worth considering, even now in 2025, if you can get the company at the right price, which I think you can in mid-November 2025.

I maintain my previously adjusted share price for the company, with adjustments based on increased volatility as well as growth potential, and point to continued upside in imaging with successes in Varian. Diagnostics remains the biggest challenge for the company, and I don't see this being solved in the near term or easily. My PT represents €53/share, with a fair value upside of 15% annualized to a price of over €65-€73/share-but even in the case of a positive Q3 or Q4, I'm not adjusting it (as I guided for in my last article), owing to some of the company's cyclicality for the short term, and other parts to tariffs and geopolitical trends.

My PT is €53/share for the native, maintained as of this article, and we're a relatively short distance from a downgrade in rating here.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative and well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this, and a price target of €53/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I rate the company "BUY" as of November 2025.

data.title: Columbia Capital Allocation Portfolios Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market overview

The third quarter saw equities reach new highs along with broadened market leadership. The S&P 500 Index and Russell 3000 Index, each a measure of U.S. equities, rose 8.12% and 8.18%, respectively. Within the U.S. equity market, small-cap stocks led the way, as measured by the 12.39% return of the Russell 2000 Index. From a style perspective, growth-oriented stocks outpaced value-oriented stocks within the large-cap segment of the market, but the reverse was true within the mid-cap segment. Within the small-cap universe, growth and value stocks performed similarly. Internationally, the MSCI EAFE Index, measuring developed market equities, returned 4.77%, and the MSCI Emerging Markets Index, measuring emerging markets equities, returned 10.64%, each in U.S. dollar terms, with the latter on pace for its best year since 2009. Fixed-income market returns were modest but positive across the quality spectrum, as concerns around a weakening labor market led the U.S. Federal Reserve to resume cutting interest rates after a nine-month pause. The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index returned 2.03%. High-yield corporate bonds and investment-grade corporate bonds, as measured by the Bloomberg U.S. Corporate High Yield Index and the Bloomberg U.S. Corporate Bond Index, returned 2.54% and 2.60%, respectively. Emerging markets debt returned 3.40%, as measured by the Bloomberg Emerging Markets USD Aggregate Index. Securitized assets, per the Bloomberg U.S. Securitized Index, rose 2.38%. In contrast to the prior quarter, long-duration U.S. Treasuries rallied, with the Bloomberg U.S. Treasury 20+ Year Index returning 2.45%. Short-term U.S. Treasury bills, per the FTSE Three-Month U.S. Treasury Bill Index, rose 1.11%. The global fixed-income market overall, as measured by the Bloomberg Global Aggregate Bond Index, returned 0.60%. Commodities returned 3.65%, per the Bloomberg Commodity Index, with gold enjoying its best year-to-date performance since 1979.

Positioning

Absolute returns in all five portfolios of the Columbia Capital Allocation Series were positive, and, on a relative basis, all five outperformed its new respective blended benchmark, attributable primarily to asset allocation decisions, especially within equity. Style decisions within equity also contributed positively across all five portfolios, more than offsetting the modestly detracting effect of style decisions in fixed income. Underlying fund manager performance overall was modestly additive in all but the most conservative portfolio, where it detracted slightly. Across all five portfolios, overall equity exposure contributed positively. Positioning and underlying fund manager performance within international equities helped most, especially our decision to overweight emerging markets equities. Overweight allocations to U.S. large– and mid-cap equities and U.S. small-cap equities also boosted results. While all segments of fixed income produced positive absolute returns, fixed-income positioning modestly detracted from relative results across all five portfolios. Having an underweight to core fixed income added value, but exposure to high yield, which is now out-of-benchmark, notably detracted from relative results across all five portfolios. Emerging markets bonds exposure also weighed on relative results in all but the most conservative portfolio. A modest use of leverage to fund an overweight in equities helped across all five portfolios. Active currency positioning also added value. During the quarter, we increased the portfolios’ exposure to U.S. large-cap equities. We trimmed the portfolios’ emphasis on growth equities to bring allocations to both growth and value equities in line with respective benchmarks. We reduced the portfolios’ fixed-income exposure overall, given our view that conditions for duration assets, such as U.S. Treasuries, remained unfavorable, but we added exposure to multi-sector high-quality credits through an increased allocation to the Columbia Bond Fund (CBFYX). A modest allocation to commodities was added in the three most aggressive portfolios.

Outlook

At the end of the third quarter, we believed investors were focused on how far the U.S. equity market could keep climbing, bolstered by artificial intelligence companies, and the degree to which the Fed may continue to cut interest rates. At the same time, markets appeared to be shrugging off trade war and tariff uncertainty. We do not anticipate an obvious catalyst to derail the bullish trend. Therefore, we intend to maintain the portfolios’ moderate overweight to equities and their underweight to fixed income, which we view as less compelling. Within equities, we maintain high conviction in our regional preferences, favoring U.S., Canadian and emerging markets equities over Europe and the United Kingdom. Macroeconomic factors support the U.S. and emerging markets positions, and quality factors support Canada and are a driver of an underweight to Europe. Strategically, we believed fixed income will serve as a useful portfolio diversifier, a view supported by lower stock/bond correlation. Within the asset class, we continue to favor higher-quality credit risk, such as investment-grade corporate bonds exposure, over high-yield corporate bonds and emerging market debt.

Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Tapasya Investment Partners as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Introduction

It's approaching three years since the fund's inception, and I'm very pleased with our collective progress. This journey has allowed me to connect with many new individuals, a significant number of whom have become investors.

Thank you deeply for entrusting me with your capital and your time. As the fund grows through both strong performance and the addition of new investors, I will always value the crucial role each of you has played in its success.

While we underperformed the market in 2024, our fund has since outperformed it (as it did in 2022 and 2023), and I am satisfied with our year-to-date results. However, it's important to remember that short-term performance doesn't tell the whole story, and true outperformance is judged over longer periods.

This year has been marked by significant news, particularly regarding tariffs, which has contributed to considerable market volatility. We experienced a rapid correction in April, followed by a historically strong market rebound.

We capitalized on some of this volatility by increasing our existing positions and initiating a new one in Interactive Brokers (IBKR). Looking back, one area where I could have improved is being more decisive. The April volatility presented several opportunities in excellent companies. Due to a combination of limited deployable capital and a degree of risk aversion, I acted less decisively than I would have preferred in hindsight.

Despite this, I am happy that we have generated a substantial lead over the S&P 500 at both gross and net (LP) levels, and I am optimistic about maintaining similar outperformance for the remainder of the year.

2025 in Review - Mid Year

At the start of the year, I expressed concerns about high equity valuations in the US, noting limited opportunities and highlighting more promising prospects abroad, particularly in Europe and China. I also saw potential in homebuilders and small-cap companies at that time.

Since my January letter, the market landscape has shifted significantly, notably with the "Liberation Day" announcement on April 2nd. This event triggered a sharp but brief market correction, which allowed us to increase our stakes in some existing holdings and initiate a new position in Interactive Brokers (IBKR).

Another change this year involves how we manage our cash. Historically, holding cash has sometimes hindered our overall returns. To address this, I decided to allocate the cash portion to a broad-based index instead. We began by holding the S&P 500 through February, later switching to KWEB, a Chinese internet stock index. This strategy aims to improve returns. In the past, our typical 5% cash allocation, intended for capitalizing on short-term market fluctuations, occasionally dampened our results. To mitigate this, we now hold this cash in a broad-based index. The potential drawback of missing out on market fluctuations is now managed through access to a line of credit.

The anticipated recovery in the homebuilder sector has not yet materialized due to persistent high interest rates. This crucial segment of the economy has been facing a recession or significant slowdown for some time due to these elevated rates and high home prices.

Our current holdings in this sector include Builder FirstSource (BLDR, -10.7% YTD), Howard Hughes (HHH, -11.7% YTD), and Fannie Mae (+173.8% YTD), collectively representing about 12% of our portfolio. I maintain a long-term positive outlook for the homebuilding industry and intend to remain invested in BLDR and likely HHH. Our investment in FNMA, however, had a shorter-term objective related to its potential reclassification from a Government Sponsored Enterprise (GSE), which could lead to substantial gains. Following positive indications, we sold approximately half of our FNMA position at roughly over four times our initial purchase price.

Over the coming months, I anticipate a moderate decrease in interest rates, assuming government spending is reasonably controlled relative to revenue. Failure to achieve this could lead to broader economic challenges, impacting not only the housing industry.

We have exited our position in Topgolf Callaway (MODG). This was part of our offense portfolio, characterized by smaller, higher-risk bets with multi-bagger potential. This investment did not perform as expected, and we sold at a loss.

The initial investment rationale was based on the potential separation of the Topgolf and Callaway businesses, following Callaway's acquisition of Topgolf in 2021. However, given the current macroeconomic conditions and management's commentary on the spinoff, the original investment thesis no longer appeared sound, leading to our decision to sell.

Below are some of the best and worst performers for this year thus far.

Top Performers and Their Contribution

Our top contributors to our performance were Prosus, Uber and Carvana. On the other hand, our bottom performers were Lululemon, Builder FirstSource and Alphabet.

A few lines in honor (or shame) for the companies that made this list.

Prosus (PRX.AS): A core, long-term holding chosen for its significant stake in Tencent. The total market value of Prosus is currently less than its Tencent holding. We believe Tencent possesses substantial growth potential and a robust global competitive advantage. Additionally, Prosus holds approximately $35 billion in other listed and unlisted assets.

UBER (UBER): Our 2022 investment thesis, centered on a confluence of positive factors driving exceptional success (the "lalapalooza effect"), has materialized as anticipated. Uber continues to execute effectively and possesses a considerable growth trajectory, leading to its long-term holding status.

Carvana (CVNA): Carvana consistently surpasses execution expectations, gaining market share and achieving profitable revenue growth. Our initial investment thesis for Carvana is also proving accurate.

Lululemon (LULU): A relatively recent addition, Lululemon is viewed as a long-term compounder. We initiated our position during a market correction related to merchandise execution challenges. Current headwinds appear to be tariff-related and are expected to be temporary (under one year). We anticipate the company's strong brand and international expansion will drive positive results.

Alphabet (Google): Alphabet presents a nuanced outlook, facing potential competition in its core search business from emerging AI technologies like Open AI's Chat GPT and Perplexity. Drawing on my marketing experience and understanding of Google's competitive advantages, a cautious yet optimistic stance is warranted, as it is too early to draw definitive conclusions about long-term search dominance. Conversely, Google's cloud business is experiencing rapid and profitable growth, bolstering its overall position. Furthermore, Waymo's progress is noteworthy, now exceeding 250,000 rides per week. Overall, we are bullish on Alphabet while closely monitoring developments in the search landscape.

Builder First Source (BLDR): Builder First Source, a manufacturer and supplier of building materials, exhibits strong capital allocation and a return on invested capital exceeding 20%. The company generates significant free cash flow, which is strategically deployed for acquisitions in a fragmented market and substantial stock repurchases (a third of shares outstanding have been retired). This is considered a long-term holding. While the housing market is currently in a recession, we anticipate strong performance upon industry recovery. In the interim, the company's execution remains reasonable.

Our ability to stay largely indifferent to mark-to-market movements in our portfolio companies when they are not reflective of underlying business issues, remains one of our competitive advantages. Our ability to withstand volatility comes from our deep due diligence and understanding of companies we own, the collective patience of our investors and our dispassionate, rational approach to investing.

Many of the largest hedge funds’ competitive advantages come from massive technology and infrastructure investments, large-scale organizations, access to information seconds and even milliseconds before others, and extraordinary trading capabilities. None of these factors is present or relevant in our approach to investing. Importantly, we believe our competitive advantages are more durable than investment strategies that rely on these attributes.

This year, we introduced 1 new positions to our portfolio: Interactive Brokers (IBKR) which I am very pleased I was able to do that. It's been a company that I was never able to buy into, to my detriment, due to valuations. During the drawdown we had this year, the stock did get cheap and allowed us to build a position. I expect to hold this for the long term. You can also find my thesis for the company in the Global Investment Review X .

As we conclude this letter, we continue to operate in one of the most complex macroeconomic conditions in recent history. While inflation levels continue to moderate, they remain above central bank targets. The prolonged tightening cycle has caused shifting expectations around rate cuts, which has created a complicated environment for markets.

data.title: Disney Q4 Earnings: Some Concerning Trends Are Emerging
data.description:
After it became clear that The Walt Disney Company's (DIS)(DIS:CA) exciting narrative around streaming has fizzled out when the company reported Q3 2025 earnings, today the company reported the last quarter for the fiscal year, and the stock is now down by almost 6% since the last report.

In the meantime, as we could see on the graph below, the S&P 500 has continued its ascend, and with that, the negative performance gap between DIS and the market is now approaching 12% in just 3 months.

On the surface the report was not quite bad, but it seems that the optimism around the stock has driven it to unsustainable levels in the short-term. Earnings on a Non-GAAP basis came-in above the analysts' estimates, but the revenue miss was noteworthy.

When looking at the results on a segmented basis, it appears that the Entertainment segment is responsible for the daily drop in the share price. Revenue within the sector was down by 6%, which is not something that investors would expect, as Disney is looking to expand profitability in streaming.

Revenue dipped 0.5% year-over-year, including gains across the Sports (+2%) and Experiences (+6%) segments, while the Entertainment segment saw revenue decline 6%.

Source: Seeking Alpha

There were, however, other moving parts, and the investors' reaction to the quarter opens up the question of whether or not current expectations are too high to justify the share price.

A Sobering Quarter

Overall, there was nothing exciting about the fourth quarter, and the sharp drop confirms that investors were betting on a sustained turnaround. Revenue for the quarter was flat year-on-year, which was not something that believers of Disney+ success were expecting.

At the very top of the earnings press release, DIS management stressed the fact that "total segment operating income" was up by 12%, which would seem like a positive development for anyone interested in the company's operating performance. However, the term "total segment operating income" is actually a Non-GAAP measure that adjusts Disney's income before income taxes with a number of items (see them in blue below). Moreover, even the adjusted operating income measure fell by 5% in the last quarter.

More importantly, however, there are some concerning trends emerging on a segmented basis. Starting with the most important segment for future growth and profitability improvement - streaming. By simply looking at the year-on-year change in revenue and profits, one could assume that margin expansion is ongoing, and with topline growth of 8%, this could have a notable double whammy effect on the bottom line.

Underneath the surface, the main factor behind the operating income increase was the higher average revenue per user, coupled with fixed cost economies of scale due to the 5% growth in subscribers. However, the vast majority of the ARPU increase occurred at Disney+ International, while Domestic roughly kept up with inflation over the period.

When we plot the quarterly changes of ARPU in both of Disney+ segments, it becomes clear that ARPU in Domestic has stayed flat for the most part of the past 12 months.

Moreover, the lower U.S. dollar has been a tailwind of the International segment which is worth considering as Disney+ now derives more sales from overseas.

By far the most concerning part, however, is the fact that the DTC margin seems to be settling at around 5%. The hype from the first profitable quarter of Q2 2024 and the initial sharp increase in margins is now over, and there is now a risk that Disney+ might not be as profitable as initially anticipated.

Bear in mind that this segment was anticipated to take over from Disney's highly profitable legacy business, and now the probability of DTC margins settling at mid-single digits appears as a very risky outcome for Disney's shareholders.

The earnings release also portrayed results in the Experiences segment as a massive success with record operating income for both the fiscal year and the last 3-month period.

Experiences: Record full year segment operating income of $10.0 billion, an increase of $723 million compared to the prior year. Record Q4 segment operating income of $1.9 billion, an increase of $219 million compared to the prior-year quarter.

Source: Disney Q4 2025 Earnings Release

When it comes to actual margins, however, the segment's performance has been relatively stable over the past two years, with quarterly margins in line with those reported in FY 2023.

In terms of growth, the optimism through the first nine months of FY 2025 has now suddenly come to a halt, with year-on-year revenue growth down to around 6%.

Disney Stock Is Not Cheap

Similarly to the Q4 2025 results, Disney's stock might appear cheap when looking at certain valuation metrics in isolation. The Non-GAAP earnings multiple, for example, appears relatively low in comparison to the P/E multiple of the S&P 500, which is now above 30.

Investors could easily be led to the wrong conclusion by looking at the multiple on a historical basis. Although the multiple below is not adjusted for Non-GAAP items, it is now much closer to what DIS was trading prior to 2019 - roughly when problems within the company began surfacing.

This might seem like a reason enough to assume that DIS is at least reasonably priced at the moment and that the market might be overreacting to what was a somewhat mixed quarter.

The main issue, however, is that Disney is no longer the company that it was prior to 2019. At the very least, the all-in bet on streaming is transforming the business, and a simple comparison of valuation multiples on a historical basis is not enough.

A good example of this is to compare the price/sales multiple and the operating income margin over time. As we could see on the graph below, Disney's sales multiple has been moving roughly in-line with the margin increases from the past few years, but the stock now trades at roughly the same sales multiple as it did all the way back in FY 2012, when margins were significantly higher. And as growth remains low, it becomes harder to justify the current multiple by anything other than the fact that the overall equity market is richly priced.

Even though peers like Netflix (NFLX) have problems of their own, Disney is no longer seen as one of the most profitable entities in the Media space. Having operating margins of around 15% is now twice as low as those of the leader in streaming - Netflix.

Lastly, there's the dividend, which some might consider an indication that things are moving in the right direction. However, since the dividend was reintroduced, the stock's yield has been way below what it used to be before the company went all-in on streaming.

Given the fact that DIS now has an annual cash outflow of around $1.8bn in dividends, it is highly likely that we will continue to see dividend increases in the coming years. The reason why this might be shrugged off by the market is because the current dividend would need to increase by at least 50% in order for the yield to approach the normal pre-2019 levels.

Conclusion

When looking at the current earnings release in further detail, we could understand why the market is reacting so negatively to the results. There are some concerning trends emerging within Disney's streaming segment, and the stock does not reflect that. Quite the opposite, the company would have to continue to deliver on the margin and dividend front for a couple more years before one could justify DIS stock as a buy.

data.title: 4 Stocks That Capitalized On The Accelerating Adoption Of AI Infrastructure
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Calamos Timpani Small Cap Growth Fund Q3 2025 Commentary.

Performance Review

For the quarter ended September 30, 2025, Calamos Timpani Small Cap Growth Fund returned 13.31% (ClassI shares at NAV) versus the Russell 2000 Growth Index return of 12.19%.

Positive Influences on Performance

Information Technology. The fund benefited from favorable security selection and an average overweight position in information technology. Positions in the communications equipment industry and the electronic equipment & instruments industry notably lifted relative performance.

Health Care. Favorable security selection and an average underweight allocation in health care added to returns, especially in the pharmaceuticals and health care supplies industries.

Negative Influences on Performance

Financials. Security selection within the financials sector dampened relative results, as holdings in the consumer finance and transaction & payment processing services industries lost ground.Industrials. Security selection and an average underweight stance in industrials, specifically in the heavy electrical equipment and aerospace & defense industries, lagged on a relative basis.

Fund Holdings - Contributors

Lumentum Holdings Inc. (LITE) 3.7% of fund as of 9/30/25

Profile: Lumentum Holdings Inc. is a leading designer and manufacturer of optical and photonic products used in telecommunications, data centers, industrial manufacturing, and consumer electronics.

Founded in 2015 and headquartered in San Jose, California, Lumentum is categorized as a communication equipment company in the information technology sector.

Analysis: Lumentum’s stock advanced 71% during the quarter, owing to a positive combination of strong financial performance, strategic growth initiatives, and favorable market dynamics. The company reported better-than-expected earnings, with revenue and earnings exceeding analyst forecasts, mainly driven by robust demand in its cloud and networking segment. Lumentum capitalized on the accelerating adoption of AI infrastructure, with record shipments of key optical components and the launch of innovative products like the R300 optical circuit switch. Strategic acquisitions and manufacturing expansion further bolstered investor confidence, while bullish analyst sentiment and technical momentum contributed to a notable rise in stock price. Broader market tailwinds and increased institutional interest also enhanced the stock’s performance during the quarter.

Rush Street Interactive Inc. (RSI) 3.1% of fund as of 9/30/25

Profile: Rush Street Interactive Inc. (RSI) is a prominent online gaming and sports betting company operating across the United States, Canada, and Latin America. The company is known for its flagship brands BetRivers, PlaySugarHouse, and RushBet, which offer real-money online casino games, sports betting, and social gaming platforms. Founded in 2012, the company is headquartered in Chicago, Illinois, and is categorized under casinos & gaming within the consumer discretionary sector.

Analysis: Rush Street Interactive’s common stock return was up 37%, driven by a combination of record- breaking financial performance, expanding user engagement, and strategic market growth. The company reported its ninth consecutive quarter of revenue and adjusted EBITDA growth, with Q2 revenue reaching $269 million—up 22% year-over-year—and adjusted EBITDA soaring 88% to over $40 million. Growth was fueled by strong performance in both online casino and sports betting segments, particularly in North America and Latin America, where monthly active users rose significantly. RSI also executed a $2.5 million stock buyback, signaling confidence in its valuation. Despite headwinds from increased taxes in Illinois, New Jersey, and Colombia, the company raised its full-year guidance, reflecting optimism about sustained momentum. Analyst upgrades and bullish sentiment further supported the stock’s 37% gain over the quarter.

Fund Holdings - Detractors

Dave Inc. (DAVE) 2.9% of fund as of 9/30/25

Profile: Dave Inc. takes its name from the “David vs Goliath” metaphor where Dave is a small, technology-focused, low-fee bank and Goliath represents the traditional, high fee big banks. Dave is headquartered in Los Angeles, California, and is listed as a consumer finance company within the financials sector. Dave was founded in 2015 and seeks to help consumers manage their finances through a variety of budgeting, liquidity, and banking services.

Analysis: Dave’s common stock declined 26%, driven by a mix of investor concerns and market reactions despite strong financial performance. Although the company reported impressive second-quarter earnings which beat analyst expectations, investors were unsettled by a rise in the 28-day delinquency rate to 2.40%, up from 2.03% the previous year. This uptick in credit risk, contributed to a sell-off after strong stock performance over the first half of 2025. Interestingly, Dave was one of our top contributors last quarter and remains in the top three among relative contributors year-to-date.

Bloom Energy Corp. (BE) (not owned)

Profile: Bloom Energy Corp. is a clean energy technology company that designs and manufactures solid oxide fuel cell systems for on-site power generation. Its flagship product, the Bloom Energy Server, converts fuels like natural gas, biogas, and hydrogen into electricity through an efficient electrochemical process without combustion. The company also offers hydrogen production solutions and serves customers in healthcare, data centers, manufacturing, and utilities with reliable, low-emission energy technologies. Incorporated in 2001, Bloom Energy is headquartered in San Jose, California, and is categorized as heavy electrical equipment within the industrials sector.

Analysis: We owned Bloom Energy early in 2025 but sold out of it as the stock struggled after the April tariff/liberation day market sell-off, and that decision looked smart through the end of 2Q, but Bloom Energy’s stock soared 254% during 3Q, driven by surging demand for its fuel cell technology to power AI-focused data centers, bolstered by a major partnership with Oracle (ORCL). Additionally, bullish analyst upgrades and price target hikes—some more than doubling prior estimates—fueled investor enthusiasm, while strong quarterly earnings and revenue growth reinforced confidence in the company’s operational momentum. Although we regrettably did not own Bloom Energy during the quarter, our other industrial sector holdings performed quite well. The relative drag from BE was the second largest individual stock for the quarter and surpassed the total relative drag from the industrials sector for the portfolio.

For additional information or to download a fact sheet, please visit the fund’s profile page: CTSIX - Timpani Small Cap Growth Mutual Fund | Calamos Investments

Summary

  • During the third quarter, small caps trounced large caps, and microcaps set the pace to beat.
  • Supported by Q3 gains, Calamos Timpani Small Cap Growth Fund (CTSIX) and Calamos Timpani SMID Growth Fund (CTIGX) remain well ahead of their benchmarks and peer averages for the year-to- date.
  • We are optimistic about the funds’ prospects—our fundamental-momentum investment style has been increasingly embraced by the market, our security selection within our framework has proven advantageous, and our nimble asset base has provided the flexibility to invest meaningfully in microcaps.
  • Sustained asset class rebounds would be the icing on the cake—nice to have but not required.

Strong earnings and a US Federal Reserve interest rate cut fueled healthy equity performance during the third quarter. Even September returns were positive despite consensus concerns relating to typical seasonal weakness. While most of our stock picks are one-off situations, we’ve also identified compelling niche themes relating to AI infrastructure, online gambling, education, advanced military technology, and securing supply chains of critical materials. Recent small-cap outperformance may have marked the end of the losing streak. Historically, the first year of a new small-cap winning streak results in an average of 2,700 basis points of small-cap outperformance relative to large caps, and the best quarter for small caps has tended to be the fourth quarter.

Analysis: We owned Bloom Energy early in 2025 but sold out of it as the stock struggled after the April tariff/liberation day market sell-off, and that decision looked smart through the end of 2Q, but Bloom Energy’s stock soared 254% during 3Q, driven by surging demand for its fuel cell technology to power AI-focused data centers, bolstered by a major partnership with Oracle (ORCL). Additionally, bullish analyst upgrades and price target hikes—some more than doubling prior estimates—fueled investor enthusiasm, while strong quarterly earnings and revenue growth reinforced confidence in the company’s operational momentum. Although we regrettably did not own Bloom Energy during the quarter, our other industrial sector holdings performed quite well. The relative drag from BE was the second largest individual stock for the quarter and surpassed the total relative drag from the industrials sector for the portfolio.

Positioning and Portfolio Changes

From a sector standpoint, the portfolio’s largest weights reside within information technology and health care on an absolute basis, while the smallest sector weights with holdings are in energy and communication services. The portfolio had no exposure to the utilities and real estate sectors. We maintain overweight allocations to the casinos & gaming and communications equipment industries. The largest industry underweight positions are biotechnology and application software.

Allocations to industrials and information technology rose during the period with increased weights in trading companies & distributors and electronic equipment & instruments. By contrast, allocations to consumer discretionary and health care decreased over the period with reductions to education services and biotechnology.
